updateMessage: Adds experimental Austria 2026 resident tax rules, including Einkommensteuer approximations, employee payroll deductions, and Austrian securities / fund wrappers.

incomeTax.name: Einkommensteuer
incomeTax.tooltip: Resident Austria ordinary-income approximation using the 2026 tariff. Employment income additionally uses a piecewise employee credit proxy that folds in the 2026 Verkehrsabsetzbetrag, the tapered Zuschlag zum Verkehrsabsetzbetrag, the EUR 132 Werbungskostenpauschale, and the tax effect of deductible employee payroll deductions. The special 13th/14th salary regime, pensioner credits, and exact tax-base construction are documented in the gap report.

socialContributions.name: Sozialversicherung und Umlagen
socialContributions.tooltip: Grouped 2026 employee payroll-deduction bucket for Austrian salary income. It combines the standard employee shares for health insurance, pension insurance, unemployment insurance, Arbeiterkammerumlage, and the common 0.5% Wohnbauforderungsbeitrag. The unemployment-insurance employee share is reduced on low monthly pay bands, so selection rules annualize the 2026 monthly thresholds of EUR 2,225 / 2,427 / 2,630. Vienna's higher housing contribution, pensioner-specific rules, and special employment categories are documented in the gap report.

capitalGainsTax.name: Kapitalertragsteuer
capitalGainsTax.tooltip: Austria taxes modeled securities gains at the standard 27.5% special rate for capital assets. Savings-account interest at 25%, detailed same-year loss-offset restrictions, and Austrian fund reporting mechanics are documented in the gap report.

helpText.label: Pension
helpText.contributionDescriptionP1:
Austria does not offer a broad employee-side personal pension deduction comparable to some other FinSim countries.

FinSim therefore keeps the modeled annual tax-advantaged private-pension contribution cap at zero unless the engine is extended for narrower Austrian pension products and employer-sponsored arrangements.
helpText.contributionDescriptionP2:
Austria does not offer a broad employee-side personal pension deduction comparable to some other FinSim countries.

FinSim therefore keeps the modeled annual tax-advantaged private-pension contribution cap at zero for both people unless the engine is extended for narrower Austrian pension products and employer-sponsored arrangements.
helpText.statePensionDescriptionP1:
Enter your expected monthly Austrian state-pension amount.

Use your latest pension estimate. Austria's actual retirement age is sex- and cohort-dependent during the women's transition to age 65, which FinSim cannot represent directly and is documented in the gap report.
helpText.statePensionDescriptionP2:
Enter your partner's expected monthly Austrian state-pension amount.

Use their latest pension estimate. Austria's actual retirement age is sex- and cohort-dependent during the women's transition to age 65, which FinSim cannot represent directly and is documented in the gap report.

investmentTypes.label: Direktaktien
investmentTypes.helpText: Directly held shares are modeled under Austria's standard 27.5% capital-income rate on securities gains. Final withholding by an Austrian bank, special rates for some interest income, and detailed loss-netting rules are not modeled separately.

investmentTypes.label: Investmentfonds / ETFs
investmentTypes.helpText: Austrian investment funds and ETFs are approximated with the same 27.5% disposal-based tax rate as other securities. The real Austrian fund regime taxes ausschuttungsgleiche Ertrage and depends on OeKB reporting / Meldefonds mechanics, which FinSim does not model and which are documented in the gap report.
